title: GDPR Audit Log Integrity and Tamper Protection

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.5(2) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.5(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.17: GDPR Audit Log Integrity and Tamper Protection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.17' running on index=_audit (Splunk audit logs), index=_internal (Splunk internal logs), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Splunk internal audit logs (_audit index), syslog integrity metadata, file integrity monitoring (FIM) events, NTP synchronisation status, and SIEM forwarding chain health checks (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform (native audit capabilities)
spl:
index=_audit earliest=-24h
| where match(action,"(?i)delete|edit_user|change_own_password|edit_roles|search")
  AND (match(info,"(?i)index.*delete|frozen|retire|capabilities|admin")
       OR match(action,"(?i)delete_index_data|edit_index"))
| eval risk_signal=case(
    match(action,"(?i)delete_index_data"), "INDEX_DATA_DELETION",
    match(info,"(?i)capabilities.*admin|role.*admin"), "PRIVILEGE_ESCALATION",
    match(info,"(?i)frozen|retire|retention"), "RETENTION_CHANGE",
    1=1, "AUDIT_MODIFICATION")
| stats count by user, action, risk_signal, info
| sort - count
| table _time, user, action, risk_signal, info, count
description: Article 5(2) requires controllers to demonstrate compliance (accountability principle). Audit logs are the primary evidence mechanism, but logs that can be tampered with have no evidentiary value. This use case monitors Splunk's internal audit trail for suspicious activities — index deletions, user-capability changes, search-time data manipulation, and modifications to retention settings — ensuring the integrity of the very evidence used to prove GDPR compliance.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _audit, _internal. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Enable Splunk audit logging (enabled by default); (2) forward `_audit` events to a separate, restricted index with extended retention; (3) alert on index data deletions, retention setting changes, and admin privilege modifications; (4) restrict `_audit` index access to compliance/security teams only; (5) consider Splunk Cloud's immutable audit trail as the source of truth for regulatory evidence. Indexes required: `index=_audit`, `index=_internal`.
visualization: Table (suspicious audit events), Timeline (audit modifications), Bar chart (events by risk signal), Single value (high-risk events today). Key fields: `action`, `count`, `info`, `risk_signal`, `user`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Log rotation during maintenance** — Scheduled log management operations (rotation, compression, archival) may temporarily modify log file metadata without altering content; correlate with the log management schedule.

2. **SIEM ingestion reprocessing** — Re-ingestion of historical logs after parser updates may create duplicate or modified entries that appear as tampering; verify against the SIEM change log.

3. **Clock synchronisation adjustments** — NTP corrections on log sources may create apparent sequence anomalies in audit trails without actual content modification; check for corresponding NTP sync events.

4. **Storage tier migration** — Logs moving between hot, warm, and cold storage tiers may change checksum values if the storage layer applies compression; verify the storage migration preserves content integrity.

5. **Log forwarding chain modifications** — Adding or removing log forwarding hops (e.g., new syslog relay) may alter transport metadata while preserving payload integrity; document topology changes.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `_audit` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.5(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.5(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.17: GDPR Audit Log Integrity and Tamper Protection." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `_audit` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Enable Splunk audit logging (enabled by default). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** forward `_audit` events to a separate, restricted index with extended retention. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert on index data deletions, retention setting changes, and admin privilege modifications. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** restrict `_audit` index access to compliance/security teams only. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** consider Splunk Cloud's immutable audit trail as the source of truth for regulatory evidence. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 24 hours, grouped by `user, action, risk_signal, info`. For "GDPR Audit Log Integrity and Tamper Protection," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `user, action, risk_signal, info` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(user) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.17: GDPR Audit Log Integrity and Tamper Protection]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.17,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `action`, `count`, `info`, `risk_signal`, `user`. Run:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, action, count, info, risk_signal, user
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.17: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`suspicious audit events`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`audit modifications`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`events by risk signal`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (`high-risk events today`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of audit log integrity and tamper protection — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Modify a test audit log entry by changing a timestamp value and verify the integrity monitoring search detects the checksum mismatch and generates a tamper alert with the affected log source and modification details.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Allow normal log rotation and compression to occur on schedule; verify no tamper alert fires for standard lifecycle operations that preserve content integrity.

evidence: Audit log integrity report showing checksum verification results, chain-of-custody metadata, and any detected anomalies with investigation status and resolution.
exclusions: Log rotation, compression, and archival operations that modify file metadata while preserving content integrity; SIEM parser updates that create reingested events with updated formatting.
